summary: Jeff Atwood argues that relying on an IDE's build command (pressing F5) instead of proper build scripts is a fundamental software engineering failure. He illustrates this through a painful email exchange showing the chaos when new developers can't build a project. The core metric he proposes is simple: if a new team member can't be productive within one day, your build process is broken. Atwood advocates for creating build scripts as living documentation, checked into source control, which then enables build servers, automated testing, and continuous integration. He acknowledges that while build servers are ideal, many corporate teams don't even have scripts outside their IDE, making that the essential first step.
key_points: How quickly a new developer can become productive is one of the most important health metrics for any software project
Relying on IDE build commands instead of proper build scripts is shockingly common in corporate development environments
Build scripts serve as living documentation that belongs in source control alongside the code
A proper build script is the foundation that enables build servers, automated testing, and avoiding 'Works On My Machine' syndrome
If your build process requires tribal knowledge or machine-specific configurations, you're accumulating dangerous technical debt
money_quotes: How long does it take for you to get a new team member working productively on your project? If the answer is more than one day, you have a problem.
If your 'build process' is the F5 key, you have a problem.
The very idea of a build script outside the IDE is alien to most of these teams.
Once you have a build script together, you've created a form of living documentation: here's how you build this crazy thing.
The F5 key is not a build process. It's a quick and dirty substitute.
If that's how you build your software, I regret that I have to be the one to tell you this, but your project is not based on solid software engineering practices.
key_insight: A reproducible, scripted build process is the foundation of professional software engineering—without it, you're building on sand and accumulating invisible technical debt.
